message.content:
I'm investigating whether this repo has enough documentation to set up a brand-new Windows host from scratch (first-time setup). Search VERY THOROUGHLY for all "windows" references, focusing on SETUP / ONBOARDING / FIRST-TIME-CONFIGURATION procedures and handbooks.

Specific places to investigate (but search beyond these too):
- The RBHW handbook family: Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_cli.sh and rbhwb_base.sh — what Windows procedures do they surface? Read them.
- Tabtargets: tt/rbw-hw* (HandbookWindows), tt/rbw-HWdd (DockerDesktop), tt/rbw-HWdc (DockerContextDiscipline) — what do they install/configure?
- Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md — read its structure/scope (ssh-to-Windows transport discipline)
- The node profile dir: rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/bujn-winpc/ — list and read what's there (BURN profile for the formal Windows host)
- Any Memos/memo-*windows*.md files — list them all and summarize each one's topic
- Any references to "first time", "onboarding", "fresh", "from scratch" near windows content
- BUK garrison / caparison / invigilate Windows setup ceremonies

For each procedure/handbook you find, report: the file path, what setup step it covers, and whether it reads like a complete standalone procedure or a stub/incomplete draft. I especially want to know if there's a partially-started handbook for first-time Windows setup.

Report findings as a structured list grouped by: (1) Setup/onboarding procedures, (2) Reference/transport guides, (3) Node profiles & config, (4) Memos. Quote short relevant excerpts (file:line). Do not modify anything — read only.

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	## RBWHAB — Access Base
2	
3	**Purpose**
4	Establish Windows as a keys-only SSH endpoint.
5	
6	**Preconditions**
7	
8	* Windows host (admin access)
9	* Network reachable on TCP/22
10	
11	**Actions**
12	
13	1. Install and enable **OpenSSH**:
14	
15	   ```powershell
16	   Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name OpenSSH.Server~~~~0.0.1.0
17	   Start-Service sshd
18	   Set-Service -Name sshd -StartupType Automatic
19	   ```
20	2. Allow port 22:
21	
22	   ```powershell
23	   New-NetFirewallRule -Name sshd -DisplayName "OpenSSH Server" -Enabled True -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -Action Allow -LocalPort 22
24	   ```
25	3. Configure `sshd_config` (typically `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`):
26	
27	   ```text
28	   PasswordAuthentication no
29	   PubkeyAuthentication yes
30	   PermitEmptyPasswords no
31	   ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
32	   UsePAM no
33	   ```
34	4. Restart service:
35	
36	   ```powershell
37	   Restart-Service sshd
38	   ```
39	
40	**Postconditions (invariants)**
41	
42	* SSH reachable on port 22
43	* Password login rejected
44	* Key-based auth required
45	
46	**Verification**
47	
48	```bash
49	ssh user@host
50	# Expect: publickey prompt; password denied
51	```
52	
53	---
54	
55	## RBWHAR — Access Remote
56	
57	**Purpose**
58	Provision client with keys and deterministic host config.
59	
60	**Preconditions**
61	
62	* Client machine with SSH
63	
64	**Actions**
65	
66	1. Generate key:
67	
68	   ```bash
69	   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/rbwh
70	   ```
71	2. Create `~/.ssh/config`:
72	
73	   ```text
74	   Host rbwh
75	     HostName <windows-host-ip>
76	     User <windows-user>
77	     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/rbwh
78	   ```
79	
80	**Postconditions (invariants)**
81	
82	* Client has dedicated key
83	* Host alias `rbwh` resolves correctly
84	
85	**Verification**
86	
87	```bash
88	ssh rbwh
89	# Expect: connects, but may not yet enter target env (routing not configured)
90	```
91	
92	---
93	
94	## RBWHAX — Access Entrypoints
95	
96	**Purpose**
97	Deterministically route SSH keys → environments.
98	
99	**Preconditions**
100	
101	* RBWHAB complete
102	* Public keys available
103	
104	**Actions**
105	
106	1. Edit `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`:
107	
108	   ```text
109	   command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l" ssh-ed25519 AAAA... cygwin
110	   command="wsl.exe -d rbtww-main"       ssh-ed25519 BBBB... wsl
111	   command="powershell.exe"              ssh-ed25519 CCCC... windows
112	   ```
113	2. Set permissions:
114	
115	   ```powershell
116	   icacls "C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys" /inheritance:r
117	   icacls "C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys" /grant "Administrators:F"
118	   ```
119	
120	**Postconditions (invariants)**
121	
122	* Each key forces a single environment
123	* No interactive shell selection
124	
125	**Verification**
126	
127	```bash
128	ssh -i key-cygwin rbwh   # lands in Cygwin bash
129	ssh -i key-wsl rbwh      # lands in WSL (rbtww-main)
130	ssh -i key-win rbwh      # lands in PowerShell
131	```
132	
133	---
134	
135	## RBWHEW — Environment WSL
136	
137	**Purpose**
138	Create canonical Linux environment `rbtww-main`.
139	
140	**Preconditions**
141	
142	* Windows admin access
143	
144	**Actions**
145	
146	1. Enable WSL:
147	
148	   ```powershell
149	   wsl --install
150	   ```
151	2. Install Ubuntu:
152	
153	   ```powershell
154	   wsl --install -d Ubuntu
155	   ```
156	3. Rename/import as `rbtww-main`:
157	
158	   ```powershell
159	   wsl --export Ubuntu ubuntu.tar
160	   wsl --import rbtww-main C:\WSL\rbtww-main ubuntu.tar
161	   ```
162	4. Enable systemd:
163	
164	   ```bash
165	   sudo nano /etc/wsl.conf
166	   ```
167	
168	   ```text
169	   [boot]
170	   systemd=true
171	   ```
172	5. Restart WSL:
173	
174	   ```powershell
175	   wsl --shutdown
176	   ```
177	
178	**Postconditions (invariants)**
179	
180	* Distro `rbtww-main` exists
181	* systemd active
182	
183	**Verification**
184	
185	```bash
186	wsl -d rbtww-main systemctl is-system-running
187	# Expect: running or degraded (acceptable)
188	```
189	
190	---
191	
192	## RBWHEU — Environment Users
193	
194	**Purpose**
195	Create deterministic multi-user Linux environment.
196	
197	**Preconditions**
198	
199	* RBWHEW complete
200	
201	**Actions**
202	
203	```bash
204	sudo adduser alice
205	sudo adduser bob
206	sudo usermod -aG sudo alice
207	sudo usermod -aG sudo bob
208	```
209	
210	**Postconditions (invariants)**
211	
212	* Users `alice`, `bob` exist
213	* Both have sudo access
214	
215	**Verification**
216	
217	```bash
218	su - alice -c "whoami"
219	su - bob -c "whoami"
220	```
221	
222	---
223	
224	## RBWHEC — Environment Cygwin
225	
226	**Purpose**
227	Install POSIX userland for orchestration testing.
228	
229	**Preconditions**
230	
231	* Windows host
232	
233	**Actions**
234	
235	1. Install Cygwin to `C:\cygwin64`
236	2. Install packages:
237	
238	   * bash
239	   * openssl
240	   * curl
241	3. Verify path:
242	
243	   ```powershell
244	   C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l
245	   ```
246	4. Ensure Cygwin's `curl` shadows the Windows-native `curl.exe`. Cygwin's
247	   `/usr/bin` must precede `C:\WINDOWS\system32` on the login PATH.
248	   Windows-native `curl.exe` cannot write `-o` output to `/cygdrive` POSIX
249	   paths (fails with `CURLE_WRITE_ERROR`, exit 23), which breaks every RBK
250	   step that writes an HTTP response into `BURD_TEMP_DIR` (OAuth userinfo
251	   discovery, most `rbuh_json` calls).
252	
253	**Postconditions (invariants)**
254	
255	* Cygwin bash functional
256	* OpenSSL + curl available
257	* `curl` resolves to Cygwin's, not Windows-native `curl.exe`
258	
259	**Verification**
260	
261	```bash
262	openssl version
263	bash --version       # expect 3.x
264	type -a curl         # /usr/bin/curl must appear first, NOT /cygdrive/c/WINDOWS/system32/curl
265	```
266	
267	---
268	
269	## RBWHDD — Docker Desktop
270	
271	**Purpose**
272	Provide Windows-hosted Docker daemon.
273	
274	**Preconditions**
275	
276	* Windows host
277	
278	**Actions**
279	
280	1. Install **Docker Desktop**
281	2. Enable WSL integration (global)
282	3. Start Docker Desktop
283	
284	**Postconditions (invariants)**
285	
286	* Docker daemon running (Windows VM)
287	
288	**Verification**
289	
290	```powershell
291	docker ps
292	```
293	
294	---
295	
296	## RBWHDW — Docker WSL Native
297	
298	**Purpose**
299	Provide Linux-faithful Docker runtime.
300	
301	**Preconditions**
302	
303	* RBWHEW complete
304	
305	**Actions**
306	
307	```bash
308	wsl -d rbtww-main
309	sudo apt update
310	sudo apt install -y docker.io
311	sudo systemctl enable --now docker
312	sudo usermod -aG docker alice
313	sudo usermod -aG docker bob
314	```
315	
316	**Postconditions (invariants)**
317	
318	* Native `dockerd` running inside WSL
319	* Users can access Docker
320	
321	**Verification**
322	
323	```bash
324	su - alice -c "docker ps"
325	```
326	
327	---
328	
329	## RBWHDC — Docker Context Discipline
330	
331	**Purpose**
332	Ensure deterministic daemon selection.
333	
334	**Preconditions**
335	
336	* RBWHDD and RBWHDW complete
337	
338	**Actions**
339	
340	1. Inside WSL:
341	
342	   ```bash
343	   docker context create wsl-native --docker "host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock"
344	   docker context use wsl-native
345	   ```
346	2. On Windows:
347	
348	   ```powershell
349	   docker context use default
350	   ```
351	
352	**Postconditions (invariants)**
353	
354	* WSL shells use native daemon
355	* Windows/Cygwin use Desktop daemon
356	
357	**Verification**
358	
359	```bash
360	docker context ls
361	docker info | grep "Server"
362	```

1	# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-16
4	Last consolidated: 2026-05-31 — promoted to the single best-memory reference
5	for Windows SSH access (accounts, privileged admin lever, current live state).
6	Prefer adding here over spawning sibling docs; a cloud of semi-healthy notes
7	is how this knowledge gets lost.
8	
9	## Why this memo exists
10	
11	When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
12	but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
13	"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
14	`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
15	failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
16	(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
17	investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
18	Log spelunking.
19	
20	The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
21	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
22	operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
23	The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.
24	
25	## Current access state on rocket (bujn-winpc)
26	
27	Verified 2026-05-31 via `tt/buw-jpS` (admin) and direct `ssh` (ad-hoc
28	accounts). This is the live picture for manual testing; the recipes that
29	produced it follow in the sections below. Tailnet host `rocket` =
30	`bujn-winpc`. All authentication is pubkey-only.
31	
32	### Accounts present
33	
34	| Account      | Reach                          | Lands in                                                                    | Role |
35	|--------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------|
36	| `bhyslop`    | `tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc <cmd>`  | Windows default shell (cmd.exe); prepend `powershell -Command` / `bash -c`  | **Privileged admin** — the lever that writes ACL-locked files |
37	| `brad`       | `ssh brad@rocket`              | Interactive Cygwin `bash --login -i`                                        | Human ad-hoc; forced-command ignores a passed command |
38	| `cygwin`     | `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` or `ssh -t cygwin@rocket` | Cygwin bash — one-shot *or* interactive login shell                         | Dual-mode Cygwin (conditional wrapper, 2026-05-31) |
39	| `wsl`        | `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` or `ssh -t wsl@rocket`       | WSL Ubuntu 24.04 as **root** — one-shot *or* interactive login shell        | Dual-mode WSL; **Docker 29.1.3 daemon live** |
40	| `bujuw_user` | `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc`        | Garrison-determined shell                                                   | Formal BURN/BURP workload account (garrison-managed) — not ad-hoc |
41	
42	The first four (admin + brad/cygwin/wsl) are the ad-hoc manual-testing
43	surface. `bujuw_user` is the formal jurisdiction path and is owned by the
44	garrison ceremony, not by hand-edits.
45	
46	### sshd posture (global, `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`)
47	
48	- `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`
49	- `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys` (default; overridden per-account
50	  by the Match blocks below)
51	- `LogLevel DEBUG3` — verbose, a debugging carry-over; harmless, revert to
52	  default when convenient (not load-bearing for access)
53	
54	Match-block map:
55	
56	| Match                  | AuthorizedKeysFile |
57	|------------------------|--------------------|
58	| `Group administrators` | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys` |
59	| `User bujuw_user`      | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/bujuw_user/authorized_keys` |
60	| `User brad`            | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys` |
61	| `User cygwin`          | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/cygwin/authorized_keys` |
62	| `User wsl`             | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/wsl/authorized_keys` |
63	
64	### Privileged admin account (`bhyslop`)
65	
66	Reached by `tt/buw-jpS` (`bujb_privileged_ssh`, pass-through) using
67	`~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin`, key-only / BatchMode. Directly observed on
68	2026-05-31:
69	
70	- Member of `BUILTIN\Administrators` and `rocket\docker-users`; High integrity.
71	- Trust is a **bare** key (no forced command) in
72	  `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`:
73	  `ssh-ed25519 AAAA…R9aZJ bhyslop@winpc-admin`
74	- ACL: `BUILTIN\Administrators:(F)`, `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM:(F)` — the canonical
75	  `administrators_authorized_keys` lockdown. The principal is the admins
76	  *group*, not a per-user grant.
77	- Authenticates via the `Match Group administrators` block.
78	- Default remote shell is cmd.exe; since the SSH path is pass-through, prepend
79	  `powershell -Command …` (or `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash -lc …`) as the task needs.
80	
81	**Origin note (observed, not replayed).** This account's *establishment* —
82	admin pubkey placement, ACL lockdown, sshd hardening — is the
83	caparison-windows Phase 1 ceremony's job (`BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc`). It
84	pre-existed this consolidation; the above is the verified current state, not a
85	re-derivation of the original setup steps. When the formal ceremony is rebuilt
86	later, that is where the authoritative admin-setup recipe belongs; this section
87	records what is true on the box today so manual testing has a complete picture.
88	
89	## The chain, in causal order
90	
91	### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile
92	
93	```
94	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
95	```
96	
97	Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
98	does NOT create:
99	
100	- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
101	- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`
102	
103	The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
104	explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
105	alone does NOT trigger profile creation.
106	
107	### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile
108	
109	The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
110	— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
111	`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
112	`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.
113	
114	For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
115	sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
116	(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
117	with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
118	the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
119	with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
120	because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
121	was attempted.
122	
123	### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup
124	
125	The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
126	path:
127	
128	```
129	Match User <name>
130	    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
131	```
132	
133	`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
134	absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
135	opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
136	service-style accounts.
137	
138	This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
139	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
140	Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
141	for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
142	spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
143	underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.
144	
145	### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix
146	
147	If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
148	session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
149	disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
150	The original directory becomes an orphan.
151	
152	This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
153	`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
154	`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
155	land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.
156	
157	Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
158	cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
159	`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
160	A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
161	garrison cycle.
162	
163	### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user
164	
165	After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
166	home paths:
167	
168	| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
169	|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
170	| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
171	| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
172	| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |
173	
174	SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
175	land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
176	session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.
177	
178	Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
179	ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
180	ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.
181	
182	Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
183	`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
184	agree on a single home.
185	
186	This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
187	during practice runs:
188	
189	```
190	ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
191	```
192	
193	If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
194	`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
195	uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
196	`/etc/profile`, no action needed.
197	
198	### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist
199	
200	sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
201	for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
202	in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
203	`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.
204	
205	Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
206	include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:
207	
208	```
209	icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
210	```
211	
212	Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
213	accepted.
214	
215	## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account
216	
217	(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)
218	
219	Admin SSH session on rocket:
220	
221	```
222	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
223	mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
224	# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
225	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
226	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
227	# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
228	#   Match User brad
229	#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
230	sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
231	Restart-Service sshd
232	```
233	
234	From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:
235	
236	```
237	ssh brad@rocket
238	```
239	
240	Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:
241	
242	```
243	mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
244	chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
245	printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
246	```
247	
248	## Substrate-routing forced-command variants (cygwin / wsl)
249	
250	The brad@rocket worked example puts the account on a hardcoded interactive
251	Cygwin shell. The same chain (account creation,
252	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/` authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also
253	supports substrate-routing personas whose forced-command wrapper encodes which
254	substrate the session lands in. Two were stood up on 2026-05-17 and, after the
255	2026-05-31 repair + broadening, both are **dual-mode** — one-shot *and*
256	interactive on the same account:
257	
258	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in Cygwin;
259	  `ssh -t cygwin@rocket` drops to an interactive Cygwin login shell.
260	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in WSL Ubuntu 24.04
261	  (as root); `ssh -t wsl@rocket` drops to an interactive WSL login shell.
262	
263	The dual-mode behavior comes from a wrapper that branches on
264	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`: empty (no command supplied → interactive intent) execs
265	an interactive login shell; non-empty execs `bash -c` with the client command.
266	Real-TTY `ssh -t` interactivity to both accounts was operator-confirmed
267	2026-05-31. The differences from brad@rocket fall in a few places.
268	
269	### Forced-command pattern
270	
271	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
272	
273	```
274	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
275	```
276	
277	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
278	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
279	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
280	
281	**cygwin@rocket.** The intuitive *inline* form is a trap. It was deployed
282	through 2026-05-31 and silently mangled every non-trivial command:
283	
284	```
285	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""   # BROKEN
286	```
287	
288	Why it fails: Windows OpenSSH runs the forced command through the default
289	shell, **cmd.exe**, which does not expand `$`-style variables (it uses
290	`%VAR%`). So bash's `-c` argument arrives as the literal token
291	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`. The single bash then expands it *during execution*,
292	and the result is only word-split — never re-parsed for shell metacharacters.
293	Net effect: the client command runs as one bare command with whitespace-split
294	argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection.
295	`ssh cygwin@rocket 'uname -o; whoami'` fails with `uname: unknown option --
296	;` because `;` is handed to uname as an argument. (Contrast wsl below, which
297	re-parses correctly — same symptom, opposite outcome, which is what made this
298	confusing.)
299	
300	The fix mirrors the wsl variant: a wrapper that adds a second bash layer, so
301	the inner `bash -c` receives a fully-expanded, re-parseable script. Wrapper at
302	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF line endings — CRLF breaks the
303	shebang/exec):
304	
305	```
306	#!/bin/bash
307	if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
308	  exec /bin/bash --login -i
309	else
310	  exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
311	fi
312	```
313	
314	The wrapper's bash is a real shell: in the command branch it expands
315	`"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` (Cygwin inherits the Windows env var sshd set on the
316	child) and hands the *value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it
317	normally; in the empty branch it execs an interactive login shell. The authkeys
318	forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the script
319	as a POSIX path:
320	
321	```
322	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh"
323	```
324	
325	ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
326	read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
327	— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
328	easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
329	testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
330	`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
331	admin.** Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z
332	A-Z'` → `HI`; interactive (no command) lands a live Cygwin `bash --login -i`.
333	
334	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the authkeys
335	command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers (the inner
336	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing, cmd.exe's
337	interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper `.cmd` file at
338	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the runtime concern. To get
339	**dual-mode** (one-shot + interactive) without re-introducing that escape
340	graveyard inside the `.cmd`, keep the conditional on the *Linux* side: the
341	`.cmd` just invokes a clean script inside the distro.
342	
343	Linux-side `/usr/local/bin/sshrun` (mode 755, inside the `Ubuntu-24.04` distro,
344	LF endings):
345	
346	```
347	#!/bin/bash
348	if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
349	  exec bash -li
350	else
351	  exec bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
352	fi
353	```
354	
355	Windows-side `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` (CRLF):
356	
357	```
358	@echo off
359	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
360	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- /usr/local/bin/sshrun
361	```
362	
363	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
364	
365	```
366	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
367	```
368	
369	The wrapper does two load-bearing things:
370	
371	- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
372	  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var. By
373	  default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the Windows ↔ Linux
374	  boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV` declaration. `sshrun`
375	  then reads it natively — no `$`-through-cmd.exe escaping at all.
376	- Routing the conditional through a Linux file (rather than an inline
377	  `bash -lc "…"` branch in the `.cmd`) keeps cmd.exe's tokenizer away from the
378	  nested quotes a conditional would otherwise need.
379	
380	The `.cmd` wrapper is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys (`SYSTEM:F`,
381	`BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`). **Caveat:** `/usr/local/bin/sshrun`
382	lives inside the distro, so a distro re-import (e.g. a future garrison
383	re-provision) wipes it — recreate it as part of any wsl re-provisioning.
384	Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh wsl@rocket 'docker --version'` → `Docker
385	version 29.1.3`; interactive (no command) lands a live WSL `bash -li` as root.
386	
387	### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation
388	
389	WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
390	`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
391	installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
392	Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
393	`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
394	elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
395	and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.
396	
397	`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
398	tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
399	because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
400	and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
401	pattern:
402	
403	1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
404	   ```
405	   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
406	   ```
407	2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
408	   location:
409	   ```
410	   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
411	   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
412	   ```
413	3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
414	   one-shot import:
415	   ```
416	   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
417	   ```
418	4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
419	   and the connection blocks until import completes:
420	   ```
421	   ssh wsl@host trigger
422	   ```
423	5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
424	   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.
425	
426	The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
427	because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
428	Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
429	modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.
430	
431	### Operational notes
432	
433	**ACL re-lock after .NET / PowerShell file writes.** `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`,
434	`[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes`, and `Set-Content` all behave the same way, and the
435	behavior differs by whether the target already exists:
436	
437	- **New file** (e.g. a freshly created `runcmd.sh`): inherits its parent
438	  directory's ACL, which does *not* include the account's own read+execute.
439	  You must apply the full lockdown including `<user>:(RX)` — otherwise the
440	  forced command runs as the unprivileged account and fails `Permission
441	  denied` reading its own wrapper. (This is exactly the 2026-05-31 cygwin
442	  wrapper miss — see "Noninteractive forced-command variants" above.)
443	- **Existing file overwritten in place** (e.g. truncating and rewriting an
444	  already-locked `authorized_keys`): the existing ACL/owner is preserved, so
445	  no re-lock is needed.
446	
447	When in doubt, the safe sequence is always: write file → (re)apply the icacls
448	lockdown → verify with `icacls <file>`. And remember the trap above: a wrapper
449	that the unprivileged account must *read* needs that account in the ACL, which
450	you cannot confirm by testing as admin.
451	
452	**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
453	content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
454	... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
455	all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
456	base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:
457	
458	```
459	B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
460	PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
461	ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
462	ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
463	```
464	
465	The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
466	file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
467	(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
468	cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
469	uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
470	`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.
471	
472	**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
473	and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
474	transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
475	the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
476	out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
477	or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.
478

1	# Windows Docker Desktop — Cygwin crucible bind-mount wall and fix
2	
3	*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). Written during the first-ever crucible
4	charge on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop host (`cygwin@rocket`).*
5	
6	## TL;DR
7	
8	Charging a Recipe Bottle crucible on an uncontrolled Cygwin box (repo checked
9	out on NTFS, Windows-native Docker Desktop on the WSL2 backend) hit **two**
10	Windows-native-docker walls beyond the already-known `docker build`/`docker cp`
11	path issues. Both are now fixed, and the bottle charges Healthy:
12	
13	1. **`docker compose` CLI path args** (`-f` / `--env-file` / `--project-directory`)
14	   were read as Windows paths, so a `/cygdrive/c/...` absolute was misread.
15	   Fixed by `zrbob_native_path_capture` in `rbob_bottle.sh` — the `/cygdrive/X/...`
16	   → `X:/...` normalizer, the **fourth** copy of the same body alongside
17	   `zrbfc_native_path_capture` (rbfc_FoundryCore.sh) and `zrbndb_native_path_capture`
18	   (rbndb_base.sh). Pinning `--project-directory` to the native form also makes
19	   compose resolve the fragment's *relative* volume mounts to Windows-native
20	   paths, which Docker Desktop's WSL2 backend needs to bind-mount.
21	
22	2. **Nested bind-mountpoint creation** failed with `EACCES`. Fixed by
23	   pre-creating the mountpoint in the bind source: tracked
24	   `Tools/rbk/rbtid/project/.gitkeep`.
25	
26	## The nested-mountpoint wall (the interesting one)
27	
28	The bottle's compose fragment (`tadmor`/`moriah` `rbnnh_compose.yml`) mounts two
29	**nested** binds, and the bottle runs as a non-root user:
30	
31	```yaml
32	volumes:
33	  - ../Tools/rbk/rbtid : /workspace          :rw   # mount A (the ifrit driver + results channel)
34	  - ../                : /workspace/project   :ro   # mount B — nested INSIDE A
35	```
36	
37	Charge error:
38	
39	```
40	runc create failed: ... error mounting ".../rbm_alpha_recipemuster" to rootfs at
41	"/workspace/project": create mountpoint for /workspace/project mount:
42	mkdirat .../workspace/project: permission denied
43	```
44	
45	**Mechanism.** A bind mountpoint must be created *in the filesystem of its
46	parent mount*:
47	
48	- A **top-level** mountpoint (e.g. `/workspace`) is created in the container's
49	  **overlay rootfs** — a real Linux fs inside the DD VM. `mkdir` there always
50	  works. (This is why the sentry and pentacle — no nested binds — came up
51	  Healthy immediately.)
52	- A **nested** mountpoint (`/workspace/project`) must be created *inside mount A*
53	  — and mount A is the **Windows-hosted** `rbtid` directory, surfaced through
54	  Docker Desktop's WSL2 host-mount gateway (`/run/desktop/mnt/host/c/...`).
55	  `runc`'s `mkdir` through that gateway is **denied (EACCES)**.
56	
57	On native Linux / WSL (ext4, no Windows gateway) the same nested bind works,
58	which is why this never surfaced before — it is specific to **(NTFS bind source)
59	× (nested bind mount) × (Docker Desktop WSL2)**.
60	
61	**Fix.** Pre-create the mountpoint so `runc` binds onto an existing directory
62	instead of `mkdir`-ing through the gateway: a tracked, empty
63	`Tools/rbk/rbtid/project/`. Cross-platform no-op — on Linux/macOS the read-only
64	repo bind overlays the empty dir; on Cygwin-DD it is the pre-existing mountpoint.
65	Proven: with the dir present, all three containers charge Healthy.
66	
67	**What does NOT work:** creating `/workspace/project` in the bottle *Dockerfile*.
68	Mount A (`rbtid → /workspace`) shadows the image's `/workspace` the instant it
69	mounts, so any image-baked nested dir vanishes. The mountpoint must live in the
70	bind **source**, not the image.
71	
72	## Implications for other projects
73	
74	- **Any container with nested bind mounts on Windows Docker Desktop where the
75	  outer mount's source is on NTFS** will hit this. Three remedies, in order of
76	  preference: (a) pre-create the nested mountpoint in the source dir (what we
77	  did — minimal, cross-platform), (b) de-nest so every mountpoint lands in the
78	  image overlay, (c) keep the checkout on the WSL2 ext4 side (the canonical
79	  Docker-on-Windows advice; avoids the NTFS gateway entirely).
80	- **APCK** (Ann's PHI Clipbuddy) bind-mounts `$HOME/apcjd/` into its container
81	  as a **single top-level** mount, and ships on macOS — so it is *not* exposed
82	  to this today. But if APCK ever nests a bind or runs on Windows DD, the same
83	  mountpoint discipline applies.
84	- **Recipe Bottle consumer credibility:** the paddock's locked "container tests
85	  run on WSL only" decision was a conservative hedge against exactly this wall.
86	  With these two membranes, crucibles now charge on an uncontrolled Cygwin DD
87	  box against an NTFS checkout — so that locked decision can be revisited.
88	  (Caveat: see `memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md` — one
89	  security sortie's *verdict* may differ on the DD-WSL2 network stack, which is
90	  a separate question from whether the crucible runs.)
91	- **Path-normalizer duplication is now 4×** (`zrbfc`, `zrbndb`, `zrbob`, plus the
92	  `rbgo_docker_login` auths bend). Consolidation into one Windows-docker adapter
93	  remains slated (heat ₣BV, step 3). The mountpoint discipline is a fifth
94	  Windows-DD membrane and should be folded into the same adapter's documentation.
95	
96	## Pale-membrane status
97	
98	Both fixes are membranes against a foreign (Docker Desktop WSL2) behavior we
99	cannot edit. Characterized (exact signatures above), contained (the normalizer
100	helpers; the one tracked mountpoint), logged (this memo + the `.gitkeep`
101	header), and given a retirement condition: retire when the mounts de-nest, the
102	foundry builds as a true Cygwin binary, or Docker Desktop fixes nested-mountpoint
103	creation on Windows-hosted binds.
104	
105	## Reference
106	
107	- WSG (Windows Scripting Guide) is the home for the Windows transport stack;
108	  this finding belongs in its orbit.
109	- Live host: `cygwin@rocket` (tailnet). Repo on NTFS at
110	  `C:\Users\cygwin\projects\rbm_alpha_recipemuster`.
111

1	# Windows Transport Experiments — OQ-1 through OQ-7
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-08
4	
5	## Context
6	
7	WSG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md`) was first
8	populated from diagnostic cycles in the predecessor pace (`₢A-AAv`,
9	correct-wsl-user-model). That work landed eight established rules but
10	flagged seven open questions (OQ-1 through OQ-7) for empirical
11	resolution. This memo records the probe matrix run against `bujn-winpc`
12	in pace `₢A-AA0` (windows-transport-experiments) that resolved them.
13	
14	Probe vehicle: `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc <command>`. Curia
15	single-quoted argv to the tabtarget so `$` reaches it verbatim; the
16	tabtarget passes argv to ssh, ssh forwards a single command string to
17	remote sshd, sshd hands it to cmd.exe (DefaultShell). From there, the
18	chain branches by which child shell is invoked: `wsl.exe ... bash` (w),
19	`C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login` (c), or `powershell -NoProfile -Command`
20	(direct PS).
21	
22	Fundus: `bujn-winpc` (BURN_HOST=rocket, BURN_PLATFORM=bunne_windows).
23	WSL distribution: `rbtww-main` (admin: `--user root`). Cygwin path:
24	`C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login`.
25	
26	Findings are summarized at the end of this memo (Summary). The body is
27	the per-OQ probe matrix.
28	
29	## OQ-1 — Which layer eats `$name` in wsl.exe transit?
30	
31	### Hypothesis (original WSG framing)
32	
33	WSG framed OQ-1 as "what eats `$()` and `$var` in `wsl.exe ... bash -c
34	"<body>"`?" with cmd.exe ruled out (SH-4) and the candidates listed as
35	wsl.exe argv parser, parens-in-quotes interaction, or OpenSSH-Win32
36	spawn side-effect.
37	
38	The matrix shows the original framing was half-right: `$(...)` is NOT
39	eaten — it survives to bash unchanged. Only `$name` and `${name}`
40	references are mangled. The eater is wsl.exe's argv parser (or its
41	argv-to-Linux-invocation transform), and the mechanism is
42	environment-variable substitution against wsl.exe's own environment,
43	not nullification.
44	
45	### Probe matrix
46	
47	#### 1A — cmd.exe alone, baseline (re-verify SH-4)
48	
49	```
50	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'echo "VAR=$X SUB=$(uname)"'
51	# stdout: "VAR=$X SUB=$(uname)"
52	```
53	
54	cmd.exe emits the body verbatim (including the surrounding `"` —
55	cmd.exe's `echo` is wholly verbatim). Neither `$X` nor `$(uname)` is
56	touched. Re-confirms SH-4 on this fundus.
57	
58	#### 1B — wsl.exe + bash, single statement, mixed `$X` and `$(uname)`
59	
60	```
61	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo VAR=$X SUB=$(uname)"'
62	# stdout: VAR= SUB=Linux
63	```
64	
65	`$(uname)` survived to bash (bash ran `uname` → `Linux`). `$X` is empty.
66	
67	#### 1C — prior cycle's symptom: `ztmp=$(mktemp); echo $ztmp`
68	
69	```
70	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=$(mktemp); echo TMPVAL=$ztmp"'
71	# stdout: TMPVAL=
72	```
73	
74	Reproduces the symptom that motivated OQ-1.
75	
76	#### 1D — replace `$(mktemp)` with `$(echo HELLO)` (rule out missing-binary)
77	
78	```
79	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=$(echo HELLO); echo TMPVAL=$ztmp"'
80	# stdout: TMPVAL=
81	```
82	
83	Same empty result. Rules out "wsl.exe ran `mktemp` Windows-side and got
84	nothing." Something's substituting `$ztmp` to empty before bash sees the
85	body.
86	
87	#### 1E — `$()` alone, single statement, no `;`
88	
89	```
90	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo SUB=$(echo HELLO)"'
91	# stdout: SUB=HELLO
92	```
93	
94	`$(...)` worked when not paired with later `$name` reference. First
95	crack in the original "`$()` is eaten" hypothesis: it is not.
96	
97	#### 1F — `;` separator, no `$` references
98	
99	```
100	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo A; echo B"'
101	# stdout:
102	# A
103	# B
104	```
105	
106	`;` works fine. Both statements ran.
107	
108	#### 1G — `;` + literal assignment + `$name` reference (the canonical failure)
109	
110	```
111	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=$ztmp"'
112	# stdout: TMPVAL=
113	```
114	
115	No `$()` involved at all. Plain assignment + bare `$ztmp` reference.
116	Empty result. **The eater operates on `$name`, not `$()`.**
117	
118	#### 1H — same body via cygwin bash (also answers OQ-2)
119	
120	```
121	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=$ztmp"'
122	# stdout: TMPVAL=HELLO
123	```
124	
125	**Cygwin works.** The differentiating factor is wsl.exe — cygwin's path
126	(cmd.exe → C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe) does not exhibit the substitution.
127	The eater is wsl.exe specifically.
128	
129	#### 1I — does `${braces}` form escape the substitution?
130	
131	```
132	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=${ztmp}"'
133	# stdout: TMPVAL=
134	```
135	
136	Braces don't help. wsl.exe substitutes `${name}` form too.
137	
138	#### 1J — `\$name` escape form
139	
140	```
141	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=\$ztmp"'
142	# stdout: TMPVAL=HELLO
143	```
144	
145	**`\$name` escape works.** The `\` survives to wsl.exe; wsl.exe treats
146	`\$` as a literal `$` (not a substitution trigger); bash sees `$ztmp`
147	literally and does its own expansion against the current shell's
148	environment where `ztmp` was just assigned. Round-trip succeeds.
149	
150	#### 1K — `$USERNAME` (Windows env var)
151	
152	```
153	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo USERNAME_TEST=$USERNAME"'
154	# stdout: USERNAME_TEST=
155	```
156	
157	#### 1L — confirm USERNAME exists in Windows (cmd.exe %VAR%)
158	
159	```
160	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'echo USERNAME_TEST=%USERNAME%'
161	# stdout: USERNAME_TEST=bhyslop
162	```
163	
164	USERNAME exists in the Windows ssh session's environment. wsl.exe did
165	NOT use it for substitution. **wsl.exe's substitution does not pull
166	from Windows env.**
167	
168	#### 1M — `$PATH` (set in wsl.exe Linux root env)
169	
170	```
171	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo PATH_TEST=$PATH"'
172	# stdout: PATH_TEST=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:...
173	```
174	
175	`$PATH` got substituted to the Linux PATH. **The substitution scope is
176	the wsl.exe Linux-side environment.** Names defined there → value;
177	undefined → empty.
178	
179	(Proof that the substitution happens upstream of bash, not by bash: 1G
180	sets `ztmp=HELLO` mid-body and then `echo $ztmp` returns empty in the
181	same bash process; only `\$` defers the lookup to bash, which then sees
182	its own assignment.)
183	
184	#### 1N — `;` + assignment, no `$` at all
185	
186	```
187	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=ZZZ"'
188	# stdout: TMPVAL=ZZZ
189	```
190	
191	Confirms `;` itself is not the culprit; only `;` + `$varname` (where
192	the name is undefined in wsl.exe's startup env) together.
193	
194	#### 1O — `;` + `$PATH`
195	
196	```
197	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "echo A; echo X=$PATH"'
198	# stdout:
199	# A
200	# X=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:...
201	```
202	
203	`;` + `$PATH` works because PATH IS in wsl.exe's startup env.
204	Substitution succeeds with the right value.
205	
206	#### 1P — full canonical escape: `\$(mktemp)` and `\$ztmp`
207	
208	```
209	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=\$(mktemp); echo TMPVAL=\$ztmp"'
210	# stdout: TMPVAL=/tmp/tmp.hNpO0X8BXI
211	```
212	
213	Canonical pattern. Both `\$(mktemp)` and `\$ztmp` reach bash literally;
214	bash performs its own expansion in its own scope. (Probe leaves a temp
215	file on the WSL distro; reaped by tmpwatch.)
216	
217	#### 1Q — `\${braces}` escape form
218	
219	```
220	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=\${ztmp}"'
221	# stdout: TMPVAL=HELLO
222	```
223	
224	`\${name}` also works.
225	
226	### Resolution
227	
228	**Mechanism**: wsl.exe's argv-to-Linux-invocation processor performs
229	`$name` and `${name}` substitution against its own (Linux-side,
230	root-shell startup) environment before constructing the bash invocation.
231	Names present in that environment substitute to their values; undefined
232	names substitute to empty.
233	
234	**Not affected**:
235	- cmd.exe (1A; matches existing SH-4)
236	- Cygwin bash (1H; this resolves OQ-2)
237	- `$(...)` command substitution (1E; the `(` after `$` does not match
238	  the `$name` / `${name}` token shape wsl.exe is looking for)
239	
240	**Escape rule**: `\$name` and `\${name}` reach bash with literal `$`.
241	
242	### Caveats and untested edges
243	
244	- Substitution semantics for `$$` (PID), `$?`, `$1`, `$@`, `$*` not
245	  explicitly tested. Recommend the same `\$` escape as the safety
246	  default.
247	- Whether wsl.exe substitutes against `WSLENV`-promoted vars vs the
248	  Linux startup env exclusively was not characterized. The rule is
249	  the same either way: escape `\$` defers to bash.
250	- Empirical for wsl 2 on Windows 10/11 as installed on rocket. The
251	  underlying behavior is plausibly a documented wsl.exe argv-quoting
252	  feature; the empirical behavior is what callers must work against.
253	
254	## OQ-2 — Does cygwin bash exhibit the same `$`-eating?
255	
256	Resolved by probe 1H above: cygwin bash does NOT pre-substitute
257	`$name`. The same body that returns empty through wsl.exe
258	(`ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=$ztmp`) returns `TMPVAL=HELLO` through
259	cygwin.
260	
261	```
262	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo TMPVAL=$ztmp"'
263	# stdout: TMPVAL=HELLO
264	```
265	
266	Consistent with cygwin's argv path: cmd.exe → Windows argv parser →
267	C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe. There is no wsl.exe intermediate to
268	substitute env vars in argv. Bash receives the body as-passed (modulo
269	cmd.exe and Windows argv-parser conventions, which do not include
270	`$name` substitution per SH-4).
271	
272	### Caveats
273	
274	- This was tested with `--login`. Whether cygwin bash without `--login`
275	  behaves the same was not separately verified; the cygwin transport in
276	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` uses `--login` so the rule covers production
277	  usage.
278	
279	## OQ-3 — Linux/Mac native ssh body semantics
280	
281	### Resolution: deferred (no Linux/Mac fundus in matrix)
282	
283	The only registered BURN profile in this regime is `bujn-winpc`
284	(`BURN_PLATFORM=bunne_windows`). There is no Linux or Mac investiture
285	available for the probe vehicle.
286	
287	Rationale for deferral being acceptable:
288	
289	- The b-letter ssh path on a Linux/Mac fundus bypasses every
290	  Windows-specific layer that produces the quirks in OQ-1, OQ-2, OQ-5,
291	  OQ-6: no cmd.exe DefaultShell, no Windows argv parser, no wsl.exe
292	  argv substitution. The remote command goes ssh → remote sshd → user
293	  shell (bash) directly.
294	- The body-as-arg shape (SH-1) is the canonical one, untouched by the
295	  Windows-only failure modes.
296	- The `$` escape rule from OQ-1/OQ-4 is wsl.exe-specific by mechanism.
297	  Linux bash via direct ssh has the bash quoting model exclusively.
298	
299	When a Linux or Mac fundus is added, run probes 1A, 1B, 1G, 1J, 1P from
300	OQ-1 (substituting `bash -c "..."` directly, no wsl.exe / cygwin
301	wrapper). Expectation: every probe behaves as plain Linux bash quoting
302	predicts; no `\$` escape needed for body-side variables.
303	
304	## OQ-4 — Canonical escape rule for body-side `$var` and `$(...)`
305	
306	### Resolution
307	
308	Drops out of OQ-1. The escape rule is per-letter:
309	
310	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name` escape | `${name}` escape | `$(...)` escape |
311	|--------|--------------------------------------------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
312	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac (deferred — OQ-3)  | (deferred)     | (deferred)       | (deferred)      |
313	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none needed    | none needed      | none needed     |
314	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`       | `\${name}`       | none needed     |
315	
316	Probe pair (verification):
317	
318	```
319	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
320	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
321	# stdout: (empty line)
322	
323	# ✅ Works on w-letter — \$ defers expansion to bash
324	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
325	# stdout: HELLO
326	```
327	
328	### Caller contract notes
329	
330	`zbujb_admin_exec` (`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:306-335`) ships the
331	body literally — caller writes the body string, the function joins
332	multi-arg form with `;` and does `"` → `\"` escaping. **It does not
333	transform `$`.** Therefore: callers calling `zbujb_admin_exec w` must
334	write `\$varname` and `\${varname}` for body-side expansion; callers
335	calling `zbujb_admin_exec c` may use unescaped `$varname`.
336	
337	The caller contract becomes asymmetric across letters. This is
338	empirically required (wsl.exe vs cygwin behave differently), not a
339	discipline choice. Workarounds:
340	
341	1. **Status quo (asymmetric)**: caller knows the letter, caller escapes
342	   per-letter. Current bujb_jurisdiction.sh callers already do this.
343	2. **Symmetric (uniform)**: have `zbujb_admin_exec` apply `$` → `\$`
344	   substitution for w-letter only. Hidden cost: callers writing `\$`
345	   for compatibility would get `\\$` after the transform; not robust
346	   against caller-already-escaped bodies.
347	3. **Symmetric via temp-file (OQ-7)**: ship body as a remote temp file,
348	   then `bash <file>`. Bypasses wsl.exe's argv substitution entirely.
349	
350	For release-1 the **status quo** is the rule of record.
351	
352	## OQ-5 — Native exit-code propagation
353	
354	### Resolution: YES, propagates reliably across all tested transports
355	
356	Probe vehicle: `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc <command>; echo "EXIT=$?"`.
357	
358	#### 5A — cmd.exe `exit 7` baseline
359	
360	```
361	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'exit 7'; echo "EXIT=$?"
362	# EXIT=7
363	```
364	
365	#### 5B — wsl.exe + bash `exit 7`
366	
367	```
368	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "exit 7"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
369	# EXIT=7
370	```
371	
372	#### 5C — wsl.exe + bash with `set -e; false`
373	
374	```
375	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "set -e; false; echo UNREACHED"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
376	# EXIT=1
377	# (no UNREACHED in stdout)
378	```
379	
380	#### 5D — cygwin bash `exit 7`
381	
382	```
383	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "exit 7"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
384	# EXIT=7
385	```
386	
387	#### 5E — PowerShell `exit 5`
388	
389	```
390	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "exit 5"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
391	# EXIT=5
392	```
393	
394	#### 5F — Native Windows binary failure via wsl.exe interop
395	
396	```
397	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "where.exe nonexistent_xyzpdq_zzz >/dev/null 2>\&1"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
398	# EXIT=1
399	```
400	
401	`where.exe` returns 1 when target not found. Exit propagates: wsl.exe
402	interop → bash → wsl.exe → cmd.exe → ssh → curia. Corroborates
403	empirical evidence cited in original OQ-5 hypothesis from cycle-3 of
404	correct-wsl-user-model: net.exe error 2224 propagated correctly.
405	
406	### Caveats
407	
408	- These probes are short-running synchronous commands. Long-running
409	  commands or commands that detach (start background services,
410	  daemonize) were not tested.
411	- `set -e` interaction with pipelines (`a | b` where `a` fails) was not
412	  separately probed; standard bash semantics apply (the pipeline's
413	  exit is `b`'s exit unless `pipefail` is set).
414	- PowerShell's `exit` from inside an `if` branch with `$null -eq
415	  $LASTEXITCODE` (the PS-1 trap) was not separately tested here; PS-1
416	  covers that case.
417	
418	## OQ-6 — Object-output flush semantics through cygwin and wsl.exe
419	
420	### Resolution: PS-2 is transport-agnostic
421	
422	The lazy-formatter discard behavior is internal to PowerShell,
423	independent of how `powershell.exe` is launched. The same `Get-LocalUser;
424	exit 0` body produces empty stdout via all three transports tested.
425	
426	#### 6A — Direct cmd.exe → powershell, lazy form (PS-2 baseline)
427	
428	```
429	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name '\''Administrator'\''; exit 0"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
430	# stdout: (single CRLF only)
431	# EXIT=0
432	```
433	
434	Reproduces PS-2 — `Get-LocalUser`'s table is eaten by `exit 0`.
435	
436	#### 6B — cygwin bash → powershell.exe, lazy form
437	
438	```
439	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "powershell.exe -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name '\''Administrator'\''; exit 0\""'; echo "EXIT=$?"
440	# stdout: (single CRLF only)
441	# EXIT=0
442	```
443	
444	#### 6C — wsl.exe → bash → powershell.exe, lazy form
445	
446	```
447	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "powershell.exe -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name '\''Administrator'\''; exit 0\""'; echo "EXIT=$?"
448	# stdout: (single CRLF only)
449	# EXIT=0
450	```
451	
452	### Side observation: PS-2 is NOT universal across all object cmdlets
453	
454	```
455	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "Get-Item C:/Users; exit 0"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
456	# stdout:
457	#     Directory: C:\
458	#
459	# Mode                 LastWriteTime         Length Name
460	# ----                 -------------         ------ ----
461	# d-r---          5/8/2026   8:33 AM                Users
462	```
463	
464	`Get-Item` (FileSystem provider's formatter) survives `exit 0`. The
465	lazy-flush behavior depends on the cmdlet's formatter pipeline, not
466	just on whether the cmdlet emits objects vs strings. This nuance was
467	folded into the WSG PS-2 rule.
468	
469	### Caveats
470	
471	- Only `Get-LocalUser` (lazy) and `Get-Item` (eager) were probed across
472	  transports. The full universe of cmdlets and their formatter cycles
473	  is out of scope; the rule pattern (use Out-String when in doubt
474	  before exit) is the safe default.
475	- The Out-String fix probe through wsl.exe was attempted but failed at
476	  the cmd.exe layer because the pipe `|` survived only one level of
477	  quoting — cmd.exe interpreted it as an external pipe and broke the
478	  body. This is a separate failure mode about pipe-in-nested-quoted-bodies,
479	  not a property of the Out-String fix itself; the fix works inside any
480	  single PowerShell body that doesn't traverse cmd.exe's pipe
481	  interpretation.
482	
483	## OQ-7 — Multi-line bodies via temp-file feed
484	
485	### Resolution: file feed works, with normalization caveat
486	
487	Two-phase ship-and-run cleanly avoids both cmd.exe newline fragility
488	and wsl.exe argv `$`-substitution. Recommended only for bodies that
489	materially exceed `;`-join's reach; SH-2 remains the default.
490	
491	#### 7A — Single-line `;`-joined body via file feed (sanity baseline)
492	
493	Phase 1 (write):
494	```
495	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "Set-Content -Path C:\Users\bhyslop\probe.sh -Value '\''echo LINE1; echo LINE2; ztmp=HELLO; echo VAL=$ztmp'\''"'
496	# EXIT_WRITE=0
497	```
498	
499	Phase 2 (run):
500	```
501	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash /mnt/c/Users/bhyslop/probe.sh'
502	# stdout:
503	# LINE1
504	# LINE2
505	# VAL=HELLO
506	# EXIT_RUN=0
507	```
508	
509	`$ztmp` evaluated as `HELLO` — bash's own expansion against the
510	assignment within the script. **wsl.exe's argv `$`-substitution does
511	not apply** because the script body comes from a file, not from argv.
512	This makes file feed a workaround for OQ-1's escape requirement when
513	the body is heavy in `$` references.
514	
515	#### 7B — Multi-line body via PS array → `Set-Content`
516	
517	Phase 1:
518	```
519	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "Set-Content -Path C:\Users\bhyslop\probe.sh -Value @('\''#!/bin/bash'\'','\''set -e'\'','\''ztmp=$(mktemp)'\'','\''echo TMPVAL=$ztmp'\'','\''rm -f $ztmp'\'')"'
520	# EXIT_WRITE=0
521	```
522	
523	Phase 2:
524	```
525	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash /mnt/c/Users/bhyslop/probe.sh'
526	# stderr: /mnt/c/Users/bhyslop/probe.sh: line 2: set: -: invalid option
527	# stderr: set: usage: set [-abefhkmnptuvxBCEHPT] [-o option-name] [--] [-] [arg ...]
528	# stdout: TMPVAL=/tmp/tmp.W0BUvegbUi
529	# EXIT_RUN=0
530	```
531	
532	`Set-Content` writes CRLF line endings by default. bash on Linux reads
533	`set -e\r` and treats the `\r` as part of the option, which fails.
534	The script keeps running because `set -e` itself never activated.
535	Output partial.
536	
537	This is a normalization issue, not a transport issue. The file is on
538	disk; cmd.exe newline fragility was avoided. The remaining problem is
539	the encoding choice on the writer side.
540	
541	#### 7C — CRLF normalization via `tr` before exec
542	
543	```
544	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "tr -d \"\\r\" </mnt/c/Users/bhyslop/probe.sh | bash"'
545	# stdout: TMPVAL=/tmp/tmp.lNbvGRvEbD
546	# EXIT_RUN=0
547	```
548	
549	CRLF stripped, body runs cleanly.
550	
551	**Caveat**: this form pipes into `bash` (no `-c` arg, no source file),
552	which means bash reads its script from FD 0. **This re-introduces SH-1's
553	stdin-consumption hazard** if the body spawns any subprocess that
554	itself reads FD 0 (notably `wsl.exe`-interop binaries). For the simple
555	probe body this is fine; for production bodies this form is unsafe.
556	
557	The safer normalization: write to a *second* file with LF endings, then
558	exec from that file. Two extra steps. Or use a writer that produces LF
559	on the first pass (PS `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText` with explicit
560	`\n`-joined content; or `Out-File -Encoding utf8` with manual newline
561	construction; or shell-side `printf` via cygwin/wsl).
562	
563	### Cleanup
564	
565	Probe.sh removed:
566	```
567	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'powershell -NoProfile -Command "Remove-Item -Path C:\Users\bhyslop\probe.sh"'
568	# EXIT_RM=0
569	```
570	
571	### Caveats (operational)
572	
573	- The probe used `C:\Users\bhyslop\probe.sh` directly — production code
574	  should use a process-unique path under `$env:TEMP` or similar with
575	  cleanup discipline.
576	- The two-phase approach is not atomic: if phase 1 succeeds and phase 2
577	  never runs, the temp file persists. Cleanup belongs in the caller's
578	  flow, ideally with a `trap`-equivalent on the curia side.
579	- Process substitution (`bash <(...)`) was not separately probed; the
580	  syntactic complexity through cmd.exe quoting layers is high, and the
581	  file-feed approach already covers the use case.
582	
583	## Summary
584	
585	| OQ | Status   | WSG promotion |
586	|----|----------|---------------|
587	| 1  | resolved | SH-6 (wsl.exe argv `$name`/`${name}` substitution; per-letter escape table) |
588	| 2  | resolved | subsumed by SH-6 c-letter row (cygwin: no escape needed) |
589	| 3  | deferred | no Linux/Mac fundus in release-1 matrix; named in WSG's "Deferred" section |
590	| 4  | resolved | subsumed by SH-6 (per-letter escape table) |
591	| 5  | resolved | SH-7 (exit-code propagation across transports) |
592	| 6  | resolved | PS-4 (lazy-flush is transport-agnostic); PS-2 tightened for cmdlet-specificity |
593	| 7  | resolved | SH-8 (multi-line bodies via file feed; CRLF caveat; no-stdin-pipe rule) |
594	
595	## Convention for future Windows-transport experiments
596	
597	When new open questions surface around the Windows transport stack
598	(wsl.exe, cygwin, cmd.exe, OpenSSH-Win32, PowerShell), file the probe
599	matrix and resolution at:
600	
601	`Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`
602	
603	Cite the memo from WSG once findings stabilize into rules. WSG retains
604	the distilled rules and one-line probe pairs; memos retain the full
605	empirical record.
606

1	BURN_HOST=rocket
2	BURN_PLATFORM=bunne_windows
